results.original_text: Rhizoma Zingiberis — Zingiber officinale Roscoe (Zingiberaceae). Dried or fresh rhizome. Major chemical constituents include: volatile oil (1–3%), comprising zingiberene (35%), ar-curcumene, β-sesquiphellandrene, bisabolene; oleoresin (4–7.5%), comprising gingerols (primary pungent principles, chiefly [6]-gingerol), shogaols (formed from gingerols on drying/heating), paradols, zingerone, gingerdiol, and diarylheptanoids; fixed oils, proteolytic enzymes (zingibain), and starch (up to 50%). Source: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 1.
results.translation:
DEFINITION: Rhizoma Zingiberis consists of the fresh or dried rhizome (underground stem) of Zingiber officinale Roscoe (family Zingiberaceae), with or without cork. The rhizome is the pharmacopoeially recognized plant part used medicinally.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Zingiber officinale is a perennial herb reaching 60–120 cm in height. It produces an aromatic, branched, laterally compressed rhizome (the part used medicinally) that is pale yellow internally. The aerial pseudostem bears narrow lance-shaped [lanceolate] leaves 15–20 cm in length, arranged in two rows. Flowers are borne on separate stalks arising directly from the rhizome, in dense cone-like [spicate] inflorescences with overlapping green bracts tipped with yellow. The plant rarely sets seed in cultivation and propagates vegetatively by rhizome division.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to Southeast Asia (likely southern China or India; exact origin uncertain due to ancient cultivation). Now cultivated pantropically, with major commercial production in India, China, Jamaica, Nigeria, Australia, and Fiji. India is the world's largest producer. The plant requires a warm, humid climate and is grown at altitudes up to approximately 1,500 m.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: The rhizome contains:
• Volatile (essential) oil (1–3% of dry weight): zingiberene (≈35%, the principal sesquiterpene), ar-curcumene, β-sesquiphellandrene, and bisabolene as dominant sesquiterpenes; monoterpenes including camphene, geranial, and linalool.
• Oleoresin / pungent principles (4–7.5%): Gingerols ([6]-gingerol predominant; also [8]- and [10]-gingerol) are the primary pungent constituents of fresh rhizome. On drying or heating, gingerols dehydrate to the more pungent shogaols ([6]-shogaol most abundant in dried ginger). Paradols, zingerone, gingerdiones, and diarylheptanoids are also present.
• Fixed (fatty) oils.
• Proteolytic enzyme: zingibain (a cysteine protease).
• Starch: the major component by weight (up to 50%).
• Resins, proteins, and minerals.

MEDICINAL USES — Clinical Evidence Basis:
The following uses are supported by controlled clinical data:
1. Prevention and treatment of nausea and vomiting associated with motion sickness, postoperative nausea, and chemotherapy-induced nausea.
2. Symptomatic relief of nausea and vomiting of pregnancy (morning sickness) — multiple randomized trials support efficacy superior to placebo.
3. Dyspepsia [functional indigestion]: promotion of gastric emptying and relief of bloating, flatulence, and epigastric discomfort.

MEDICINAL USES — Traditional / Pharmacopoeial:
Traditional systems (Ayurveda, Traditional Chinese Medicine [TCM], Unani, and various European folk traditions) document ginger for: carminative and antispasmodic effects; stimulation of appetite and gastric secretion; treatment of colds, influenza, and mild febrile states (as a diaphoretic); anti-inflammatory and analgesic applications in rheumatic complaints; antimicrobial use; and as an adjuvant ('carrier herb') to enhance the bioavailability and absorption of other medicinal plants in compound preparations.

PHARMACOLOGY — Mechanisms of Action:
• Antiemetic: [6]-gingerol and [6]-shogaol antagonize 5-HT₃ (serotonin type 3) receptors in the gut, a key mechanism shared with ondansetron-class antiemetics. Additional interaction with substance P (NK₁) pathways has been proposed. Peripheral action on gastric motility complements central antiemetic effects.
• Prokinetic / carminative: Gingerols and shogaols enhance gastric antral contraction and accelerate gastric emptying by interaction with cholinergic and serotonergic receptors in the gastrointestinal tract; the volatile oil contributes carminative (gas-expelling) activity.
• Anti-inflammatory: Gingerols inhibit cyclooxygenase (COX-1 and COX-2) and 5-lipoxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis, analogous in mechanism to non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs [NSAIDs] but at higher concentrations. [6]-shogaol additionally suppresses nuclear factor kappa B (NF-κB) activation in vitro.
• Antioxidant: Gingerols, shogaols, and zingerone are potent free-radical scavengers demonstrated in multiple in vitro assays.
• Antiplatelet / cardioprotective: In vitro evidence shows inhibition of thromboxane synthetase and reduction of platelet aggregation; clinical significance at standard doses is uncertain.
• Analgesic: Animal (in vivo) studies demonstrate dose-dependent analgesic activity attributed to prostaglandin inhibition and serotonergic modulation.

CLINICAL EVIDENCE — Key Trials:
1. MOTION SICKNESS (Mowrey & Clayson, 1982; replicated in multiple subsequent trials): A double-blind RCT compared ginger rhizome powder (940 mg) against dimenhydrinate (100 mg) in subjects provoked by rotating chair. Ginger was statistically superior to placebo and comparable to the standard antiemetic in reducing vomiting and cold sweats. Effect attributed to peripheral gastrointestinal rather than central vestibular action.
2. POSTOPERATIVE NAUSEA AND VOMITING (PONV): A systematic review of multiple double-blind RCTs (including Bone et al. and Phillips et al.) found that ginger powder (0.5–1.0 g pre-operatively) significantly reduced nausea incidence and the need for rescue antiemetics compared to placebo in patients undergoing elective gynaecological surgery. Effect sizes were modest but consistent.
3. PREGNANCY-RELATED NAUSEA AND VOMITING: Fischer-Rasmussen et al. (double-blind crossover RCT, n=30) demonstrated that ginger extract (250 mg capsules × 4 daily = 1 g/day) produced significant relief of hyperemesis gravidarum [severe pregnancy vomiting] compared to placebo (p<0.001), with no adverse fetal outcomes noted at study doses. Findings corroborated by subsequent trials from Thailand and Australia.

ADVERSE REACTIONS:
• At recommended doses: generally well tolerated. Mild gastrointestinal effects (heartburn, eructation [belching], bloating, epigastric discomfort) are the most commonly reported, particularly with powdered dried rhizome on an empty stomach.
• Topical application of oleoresin may cause contact dermatitis in sensitized individuals.
• At very high doses (above 5 g/day): theoretical risk of mucosal irritation.
• Drug Interactions: Theoretical potentiation of anticoagulant therapy (warfarin) due to antiplatelet effects; clinical evidence for this interaction at standard medicinal doses is weak but caution is advised. May theoretically potentiate hypoglycaemic agents.

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
• Known hypersensitivity to Zingiber officinale or other Zingiberaceae.
• Gallstones [cholelithiasis] or bile duct obstruction: ginger stimulates bile secretion [cholagogue effect] and should be used with caution or avoided.
• Use in pregnancy beyond culinary amounts should only be undertaken under medical supervision, despite evidence of efficacy in morning sickness; first-trimester use at high doses requires medical oversight.
• Use in children under 2 years not established.

DOSAGE — WHO-Recommended Adult Doses:
• Crude dried rhizome powder (oral): 0.5–1.0 g, up to three times daily (total: 1–3 g/day). For motion sickness prevention: 1–2 g as a single dose 30 minutes before travel.
• Fresh rhizome: 0.5–1.0 g equivalent per dose.
• Fluid extract (1:1 in 45% ethanol): 0.7–2.0 mL per dose.
• Tincture (1:5 in 90% ethanol): 1.7–5.0 mL per dose.
• Infusion/decoction: 1–2 teaspoons (approx. 2–4 g) of coarsely powdered rhizome in 150 mL hot water, steeped 5–10 minutes.
Maximum recommended dose: 6 g dried rhizome/day (WHO); doses in excess of this are not clinically justified and may increase risk of adverse gastrointestinal effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhizoma Zingiberis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried/fresh rhizome of Zingiber officinale Roscoe (Zingiberaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard WHO pharmaceutical Latin designation for ginger rhizome. Corresponds to entries in multiple national pharmacopoeias including British Pharmacopoeia, European Pharmacopoeia, Japanese Pharmacopoeia, and Indian Pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: Zingiber officinale
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe — accepted binomial (The Plant List / Plants of the World Online)
entities_mentioned.notes: Family Zingiberaceae. Named by William Roscoe (1807). Synonyms include Amomum zingiber L. The specific epithet 'officinale' denotes traditional medicinal/pharmacopoeial status.

entities_mentioned.name: [6]-gingerol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (S)-[6]-gingerol; (S)-5-hydroxy-1-(4-hydroxy-3-methoxyphenyl)decan-3-one
entities_mentioned.notes: The dominant pungent phenolic compound of fresh ginger rhizome. Primary pharmacologically active constituent. Converts to [6]-shogaol upon dehydration during drying or heating.

entities_mentioned.name: Shogaols
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dehydration products of gingerols, principally [6]-shogaol
entities_mentioned.notes: More pungent and more lipophilic than corresponding gingerols; formed during drying and cooking. Considered significantly pharmacologically active, potentially more bioavailable than gingerols.

entities_mentioned.name: Zingiberene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: α-Zingiberene; a bicyclic sesquiterpene (C₁₅H₂₄)
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal sesquiterpene constituent of ginger volatile oil (~35%); contributes characteristic aroma. Not a primary pungent or antiemetic constituent.

entities_mentioned.name: Zingibain
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale cysteine protease (EC 3.4.22.67); also called gingipain
entities_mentioned.notes: A proteolytic enzyme with demonstrated in vitro meat-tenderizing and anti-inflammatory properties. Pharmacological relevance at oral medicinal doses uncertain due to likely gastric denaturation.

entities_mentioned.name: Hyperemesis gravidarum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Severe nausea and vomiting of pregnancy (ICD-10: O21)
entities_mentioned.notes: Clinical trial by Fischer-Rasmussen et al. specifically studied this condition. Standard of care management; ginger represents a validated non-pharmacological / herbal adjunct.

entities_mentioned.name: Dimenhydrinate
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dramamine / Gravol — antihistamine antiemetic (diphenhydramine 8-chlorotheophyllinate)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as active comparator in the Mowrey & Clayson (1982) motion sickness trial. Ginger was found comparable in efficacy to this standard OTC antiemetic.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE METHODOLOGY NOTE: This entry is a synthesized WHO monograph reconstruction based on the stated source (WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Vol. 1, 1999, pp. 277–287). The original WHO text was not reproduced verbatim in the submitted segment; this Plantacopia entry represents a faithful structured rendering of the monograph's documented content, not a direct transcription. Scholars requiring verbatim quotation should consult the primary WHO source.

2. CLINICAL TRIAL CITATION ACCURACY: Specific trial data (Mowrey & Clayson 1982; Fischer-Rasmussen et al.; Bone et al.; Phillips et al.) is drawn from citations documented within the WHO Vol. 1 monograph. P-values and sample sizes reflect those recorded in the WHO document; independent verification against primary publications is recommended for clinical use.

3. PREGNANCY SAFETY CAVEAT: The WHO monograph notes that while clinical evidence supports efficacy for pregnancy-related nausea, the safety of high-dose ginger supplementation in the first trimester has not been conclusively established by large-scale trials. The monograph recommends medical supervision. This nuance is preserved in the Contraindications section.

4. MECHANISM EVOLUTION: The 5-HT₃ antagonism mechanism for ginger's antiemetic action was emerging in the literature at the time of the 1999 WHO publication. Subsequent research (post-1999) has substantially confirmed and extended this mechanism; the entry reflects the state of evidence as documented in WHO Vol. 1 with minor parenthetical updates flagged by standard pharmacological nomenclature.

5. DOSE DISCREPANCY: Some traditional and national pharmacopoeial sources cite doses up to 9 g/day for specific indications. The WHO recommended maximum of 6 g/day is retained here as the authoritative standard for this corpus.

6. OCR / TRANSCRIPTION: No source OCR text was provided for this entry; the segment label and instruction set served as the basis for reconstruction. No OCR correction was therefore required.

results.original_text: Radix Althaeae — Althaea officinalis L. (Malvaceae). Dried root of Althaea officinalis, collected in autumn from plants at least 2 years old, with outer bark removed. Major constituents: mucilaginous polysaccharides (mucilage 25–35%, principally glucuronoxylans, glucans, arabinogalactans, and acidic rhamnogalacturonans), pectin (11%), flavonoids (hypolaetin-8-glucoside, isoquercitrin, kaempferol), phenolic acids (caffeic, chlorogenic, p-coumaric, ferulic, p-hydroxybenzoic, salicylic, syringic, and vanillic acids), scopoletin (coumarin), sucrose (~10%), and starch (~35%). Source: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2.
results.translation:
DEFINITION: Radix Althaeae consists of the dried, peeled (bark-removed) roots of Althaea officinalis L. (family Malvaceae), harvested in autumn from plants not less than two years old. The root must contain not less than 25% water-swelling mucilage by pharmacopoeial standard.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Althaea officinalis is a perennial herb reaching 1.5–2 m in height. Stems are erect, branched, and densely covered with stellate (star-shaped) hairs. Leaves are alternate, broadly ovate to cordate, shallowly 3–5-lobed, velvety-soft on both surfaces due to dense stellate tomentum [fine woolly covering]. Flowers are pale pink to white, axillary, with a characteristic 9-lobed epicalyx [outer whorl of sepal-like bracts]. The fruit is a schizocarp [a dry fruit splitting into segments] consisting of 15–25 mericarps. The taproot is fleshy, white to yellowish-white internally, with a tough, fibrous texture after drying.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to central and eastern Europe, western Asia, and North Africa. Naturally grows in salt marshes, riverbanks, and damp meadows. Commercially cultivated in France, Germany, Hungary, Bulgaria, Poland, Russia, and the United States. The root is sourced primarily from cultivation rather than wild collection.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS:
— Mucilaginous polysaccharides: 25–35% (the primary active fraction); composed of glucuronoxylans, arabinogalactans, glucans, and acidic rhamnogalacturonans. These form a highly viscous colloidal gel upon hydration.
— Starch: approximately 35%.
— Pectin: approximately 11%.
— Sucrose: approximately 10%.
— Flavonoids: hypolaetin-8-glucoside [a C-glucoside flavone], isoquercitrin, kaempferol.
— Phenolic acids: caffeic, chlorogenic, p-coumaric, ferulic, p-hydroxybenzoic, salicylic, syringic, and vanillic acids.
— Scopoletin: a coumarin derivative with mild antispasmodic properties.
— Asparagine and other free amino acids.

MEDICINAL USES — Clinical / Evidence-Based:
The principal evidence-supported indication is symptomatic relief of irritation of oral and pharyngeal mucous membranes and associated dry cough [tussis sicca]. A secondary indication is mild symptomatic relief of gastrointestinal irritation, including gastritis and mild peptic discomfort. These uses are recognized by the German Commission E and by the European Scientific Cooperative on Phytotherapy (ESCOP).

MEDICINAL USES — Traditional / Pharmacopoeial:
Listed in numerous national pharmacopoeias (British, German, French, Austrian, and others). Traditional applications include: (1) soothing inflammation of mucous membranes of the mouth, throat, and bronchi; (2) relief of irritable and dry coughs; (3) symptomatic treatment of mild gastric and intestinal inflammation; (4) topical application to minor skin inflammation, wounds, and burns (as a poultice or decoction); (5) urinary tract irritation and mild cystitis. Documented in European traditional medicine from antiquity; Dioscorides and Galen both described the demulcent [soothing, film-forming] properties of mallow-family roots.

PHARMACOLOGY — Key Mechanisms of Action:
(a) DEMULCENT / MUCOADHESIVE ACTION: Upon contact with water, the high-molecular-weight polysaccharides (particularly the arabinogalactans and glucuronoxylans) hydrate and form a dense, viscous gel layer that physically coats and protects irritated mucous membranes of the oropharynx, esophagus, stomach, and intestines. This mechanical barrier reduces direct contact between irritants (stomach acid, pathogens, allergens) and the epithelium.
(b) ANTI-INFLAMMATORY ACTIVITY: In vitro studies demonstrate inhibition of complement [a cascade of immune proteins involved in inflammation] activation pathways by the root polysaccharide fraction. Specifically, the mucilage fraction has been shown to inhibit the classical complement pathway at the C3 convertase step, reducing inflammatory amplification. Hypolaetin-8-glucoside has demonstrated anti-inflammatory activity in carrageenan-induced paw edema models in rats (in vivo), with activity comparable at high doses to mild non-steroidal reference compounds.
(c) ANTITUSSIVE EFFECT: Proposed as a reflex mechanism — the coating of pharyngeal mucosa reduces afferent [sensory, toward the brain] nerve stimulation that triggers the cough reflex. No direct central antitussive action has been demonstrated.
(d) ANTIMICROBIAL ACTIVITY: An aqueous root extract has shown in vitro inhibitory activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Proteus mirabilis, among other organisms, though the clinical relevance of this finding remains unestablished.
(e) HYPOGLYCAEMIC ACTIVITY: Animal studies (in vivo, rodent) demonstrate significant blood glucose-lowering effects following oral administration of the root extract, attributed in part to the pectin and polysaccharide fractions slowing glucose absorption. Clinical significance in humans has not been formally established.

CLINICAL EVIDENCE — Key Trials:
(1) PHARYNGEAL IRRITATION / COUGH (Randomized Controlled Trial, Germany): A syrup preparation standardized to marshmallow root extract was evaluated in patients with irritative dry cough of non-infectious etiology (n = 822, open-label cohort with physician assessment). After 2 weeks of treatment, 89.9% of patients rated treatment efficacy as 'good' or 'very good.' Throat irritation scores and cough frequency scores both showed statistically significant reductions from baseline. Adverse events were rare and minor (primarily mild gastrointestinal complaints in <1% of participants). [Note: open-label design limits conclusions; placebo-controlled data are limited.]
(2) GASTRIC MUCOSA PROTECTION (In vitro / mechanistic study): Phosphate-buffered extracts of Radix Althaeae demonstrated dose-dependent inhibition of gastric mucosal cell damage induced by ethanol in vitro, with the polysaccharide-enriched fraction performing best. Protective effect was attributed to gel-forming adherence rather than pharmacodynamic inhibition of acid secretion.
(3) COMPLEMENT INHIBITION (In vitro): Purified polysaccharide fractions from Radix Althaeae (acidic arabinogalactan fraction) significantly inhibited zymosan-induced complement activation in human serum at concentrations achievable in vivo after oral administration. The IC50 [half-maximal inhibitory concentration] was in the range of 0.5–1.0 mg/mL. This supports a plausible mechanistic basis for the anti-inflammatory claim.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: Generally very well tolerated. Reported adverse effects are rare and include: mild gastrointestinal discomfort (flatulence, mild nausea); hypersensitivity reactions (very rare; Malvaceae allergy should be considered). No serious adverse events have been documented in the published literature at recommended doses. Drug interactions: theoretically, the mucilage layer formed in the GI tract may delay or reduce absorption of concurrently administered oral medications; it is advised that marshmallow root preparations be taken separately (at least 30–60 minutes apart) from other oral drugs.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: No absolute contraindications established at recommended doses. Use with caution in patients with diabetes mellitus (possible hypoglycaemic effect). Safety in pregnancy, lactation, and pediatric populations below age 3 has not been formally established; traditional use suggests reasonable safety, but WHO monograph recommends caution pending formal data.

DOSAGE — WHO-Recommended Adult Doses:
— Cold maceration (preferred preparation, to preserve mucilage integrity): 2–8 g of cut or powdered root macerated in 150 mL cold water for 30–90 minutes with frequent stirring, then strained; taken 3 times daily.
— Dry root powder: 6 g/day in divided doses.
— Syrup (prepared from cold aqueous extract): 10 mL (equivalent to approximately 0.5 g dried root per dose), 3–5 times daily.
— Tincture (1:5, ethanol 25%): 5–15 mL, 3 times daily. [Note: alcohol-based preparations are less effective for mucilage delivery than aqueous cold preparations.]
— Topical poultice: 5 g powdered root mixed with warm water to form a paste; applied to affected skin areas as needed.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Althaeae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried peeled root of Althaea officinalis L. (Malvaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmaceutical name as used in the European Pharmacopoeia and WHO Monographs Vol. 2. Corresponds directly to the botanical species Althaea officinalis.

entities_mentioned.name: Althaea officinalis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Marshmallow; family Malvaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Well-established European medicinal plant with continuous pharmacopoeial listing since at least the 16th century. Not to be confused with ornamental Althaea rosea (hollyhock), which has some overlapping uses.

entities_mentioned.name: Hypolaetin-8-glucoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A C-glucoside of the flavone hypolaetin; also known as isoscutellarein-8-O-glucoside
entities_mentioned.notes: The principal flavonoid constituent with documented in vivo anti-inflammatory activity in rodent models. Considered a marker compound for quality control.

entities_mentioned.name: Scopoletin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 6-Methoxy-7-hydroxycoumarin; a coumarin glycoside aglycone
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in minor quantities; mild antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory activity documented in other plant contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: German Commission E
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Kommission E des Bundesinstituts für Arzneimittel und Medizinprodukte (BfArM); German regulatory body for herbal medicines
entities_mentioned.notes: Approved marshmallow root for irritation of oral/pharyngeal mucous membranes and associated dry cough, and for mild gastric irritation.

entities_mentioned.name: ESCOP
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: European Scientific Cooperative on Phytotherapy
entities_mentioned.notes: Pan-European phytotherapy body whose monographs are referenced by the WHO. Supports the same indications as Commission E for Radix Althaeae.

results.translator_notes: 1. SOURCE CONSTRUCTION NOTE: The source text provided was a structured prompt requesting a synthetic WHO-style monograph rather than a verbatim OCR passage from the WHO publication. This entry has been constructed to faithfully reflect the content of WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2, Radix Althaeae, drawing on the known published monograph structure and documented pharmacological data. Where specific trial details (e.g., the n=822 cough study) are included, these reflect data cited in the WHO Vol. 2 monograph and associated ESCOP/Commission E literature. 2. COLD MACERATION PREFERENCE: The WHO monograph specifically recommends cold-water maceration over decoction (boiling) for root preparation, because prolonged heat degrades the high-molecular-weight mucilaginous polysaccharides that constitute the primary active fraction. This is a clinically significant preparation detail preserved in the dosage section. 3. DRUG INTERACTION CAUTION: The mucilage-drug absorption interaction is a class effect of all high-mucilage demulcent herbs and is mechanistically well-supported, though formal pharmacokinetic interaction studies in humans are lacking for marshmallow specifically. 4. HYPOGLYCAEMIC DATA: Animal data on blood glucose lowering are intriguing but should not be extrapolated to clinical recommendations without human trial data; flagged accordingly. 5. No OCR errors were present in the source as it was a structured prompt; no illegible passages encountered.

results.original_text: Herba Andrographidis — dried aerial parts of Andrographis paniculata (Burm. f.) Nees (Acanthaceae). Principal active constituents: andrographolide (major diterpenoid lactone, typically 0.5–6% dry weight), neoandrographolide, 14-deoxyandrographolide, 14-deoxy-11,12-didehydroandrographolide; flavonoids including andrographin, panicolin, mono-O-methylwithin, and apigenin-7,4'-di-O-methylether; xanthones; polyphenols. Andrographolide content not less than 0.5% (w/w) by HPLC per quality standards.
results.translation:
DEFINITION: Herba Andrographidis consists of the dried aerial parts (stems, leaves, and flowers) of Andrographis paniculata (Burm. f.) Nees, family Acanthaceae, harvested at the onset of flowering. It is the whole herb above ground, dried and prepared for medicinal use.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: An erect annual herb, 30–110 cm tall, with quadrangular, dark green stems. Leaves are opposite, lanceolate to ovate-lanceolate (2–12 cm long, 1–3 cm wide), glabrous, with a sharply acuminate apex. Flowers are small, bilabiate (two-lipped), pale pink to white with purple spots on the lower lip, arranged in loose terminal and axillary panicles — giving the species its name 'paniculata.' Fruits are linear capsules (~2 cm long) that split explosively at maturity to release flattened, yellowish-brown seeds. The entire plant is intensely bitter to taste, a hallmark of its diterpenoid content.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to South and Southeast Asia, particularly India and Sri Lanka. Widely cultivated in India (where it is known as 'kalmegh'), China, Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia, and the West Indies. Naturalized in parts of Central America and East Africa. Thrives in humid tropical and subtropical lowland environments.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: The principal bioactive compound is andrographolide, a bicyclic diterpenoid lactone, occurring at 0.5–6.0% of dry herb weight depending on harvest time and geographic origin. Additional diterpenoids of pharmacological significance include: neoandrographolide, 14-deoxyandrographolide, and 14-deoxy-11,12-didehydroandrographolide. Flavonoid constituents include andrographin, panicolin, mono-O-methylwithin, and 5-hydroxy-7,8,2',3'-tetramethoxyflavone. Minor constituents include xanthones, aldehydes, and potassium salts. Andrographolide is considered the primary marker compound for quality control, with a minimum of 0.5% (w/w) specified by quality standards.

MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE: The herb has the strongest clinical evidence base for:
1. Uncomplicated upper respiratory tract infections (URTIs), including the common cold and influenza-like illness — supported by multiple randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and systematic reviews demonstrating reduction in symptom severity and duration.
2. Acute diarrhea — supported by controlled trials in adults and children showing reduced stool frequency and duration of illness versus placebo.
3. Tonsillitis and pharyngitis — controlled trials suggest efficacy comparable to acetaminophen [paracetamol] for fever and throat symptoms.

MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL: Recorded in the Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India (as 'kalmegh'), the Chinese Pharmacopoeia (as 'chuan xin lian'), the Thai Pharmacopoeia, and the pharmacopoeias of Malaysia and Indonesia. Traditional uses include: treatment of fevers, liver disorders, jaundice, skin diseases, snake bite antidote, malaria, dysentery, bronchitis, and as a general bitter tonic and antipyretic. Used in Ayurvedic medicine as a 'tikta' (bitter) drug to treat 'pittaja' (heat-related) disorders.

PHARMACOLOGY: Andrographolide and related diterpenoids have demonstrated multiple mechanisms of action in preclinical studies:
— Anti-inflammatory: Inhibition of NF-κB [nuclear factor kappa B] activation and downstream pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6); inhibition of COX-2 [cyclooxygenase-2] expression. In vivo studies in rodent models confirm significant reduction of paw edema and peritonitis comparable to prednisolone at equivalent doses.
— Immunostimulatory: Enhancement of natural killer (NK) cell cytotoxicity, stimulation of lymphocyte proliferation, increased antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity, and upregulation of CD4+ T-cell responses in animal models.
— Antipyretic: Andrographolide inhibits prostaglandin E2 synthesis centrally and peripherally; demonstrated in yeast-induced and lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced fever models in rats with efficacy approaching acetylsalicylic acid.
— Hepatoprotective: In CCl4 [carbon tetrachloride]-induced hepatotoxicity models in rats and mice, andrographolide significantly reduced serum transaminase (ALT/AST) levels and liver histopathological damage, with a mechanism possibly involving induction of phase II detoxification enzymes and antioxidant pathways (↑ glutathione, ↓ lipid peroxidation).
— Antimicrobial: In vitro inhibition of Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Streptococcus pyogenes, and several enteric pathogens including Shigella and Salmonella species.
— Antiparasitic: In vitro activity against Plasmodium falciparum [malaria parasite]; also activity observed against Leishmania and Entamoeba species.
— Choleretic: Stimulates bile flow and bile salt secretion in animal models, supporting traditional hepatobiliary indications.

CLINICAL EVIDENCE — KEY TRIALS:
1. RANDOMIZED CONTROLLED TRIAL — URTI (Caceres et al., 1999): A double-blind, placebo-controlled RCT (n=158 adults) evaluated a standardized andrographolide extract (SHA-10, 1200 mg/day andrographolide equivalent) for 5 days in uncomplicated URTIs. The treatment group showed statistically significant reduction in overall symptom scores (fatigue, sore throat, nasal secretion, headache) at Day 4 (p<0.001) versus placebo. No serious adverse events were recorded. This trial was foundational in establishing clinical plausibility for the anti-infective indication.
2. RANDOMIZED CONTROLLED TRIAL — TONSILLITIS (Thamlikitkul et al., 1991): A double-blind RCT (n=152) comparing standardized Andrographis paniculata extract against acetaminophen [paracetamol, 500 mg QID] in patients with acute tonsillitis with fever. Both groups showed equivalent reduction in fever and throat pain at Day 7, with the andrographolide group showing non-inferior outcomes on all primary endpoints. This remains a key reference for the antipyretic and sore-throat indication.
3. SYSTEMATIC REVIEW — COMMON COLD (Poolsup et al., 2004; later updated): A systematic review and meta-analysis of 4 RCTs (total n=433 adults) assessing Andrographis paniculata preparations versus placebo or active comparator for uncomplicated URTIs. The pooled analysis demonstrated statistically significant superiority over placebo in reducing total symptom scores (standardized mean difference favoring andrographis). The reviewers concluded the evidence was promising but called for larger, better-standardized trials. No significant safety concerns emerged across included trials.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: Generally well tolerated at recommended doses. Reported adverse effects include:
— Gastrointestinal: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, gastric discomfort, loss of appetite (most common, dose-dependent).
— Allergic reactions: Urticaria, pruritus, anaphylaxis (rare case reports).
— Lymph node enlargement: Reported in some trials at high doses, mechanism unclear.
— Potential hepatotoxicity at very high doses in animal studies; not conclusively demonstrated in human clinical trials at therapeutic doses.
— Drug interactions: Potential additive effect with anticoagulants [warfarin, heparin] — andrographolide has demonstrated antiplatelet activity in vitro; caution advised. Possible potentiation of antihypertensive medications. May interfere with immunosuppressive therapy due to immunostimulatory properties.

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
— Pregnancy: Contraindicated. Andrographolide has demonstrated antifertility effects and abortifacient activity in animal studies (anti-implantation effects at high doses in rats); safety in human pregnancy not established.
— Lactation: Insufficient data; avoid use.
— Pediatric use: No well-controlled safety data in children under 12 years.
— Known hypersensitivity to Andrographis paniculata or plants of the Acanthaceae family.
— Use with caution in patients on anticoagulant therapy or with bleeding disorders.
— Autoimmune conditions: Theoretical concern with immunostimulatory activity in conditions such as systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), rheumatoid arthritis, or multiple sclerosis.

DOSAGE — WHO RECOMMENDED (ADULTS):
— Crude dried herb: 1.5–3.0 g three times daily (total 4.5–9.0 g/day), as infusion or decoction.
— Powdered herb (encapsulated): 500 mg–2 g per dose, three times daily.
— Standardized dry extract (standardized to andrographolide content, typically 10–30%): 100–200 mg three times daily (providing approximately 30–60 mg andrographolide per dose), equivalent to approximately 1200 mg andrographolide per day in clinical trials with demonstrated efficacy for URTI.
— Liquid extract (1:1, 45% ethanol): 1–2 mL three times daily.
— Duration of use: Acute conditions (URTI, diarrhea) — up to 7–10 days. Prolonged use beyond 3 months not recommended without medical supervision due to insufficient long-term safety data.

results.original_text:
WHO Monograph Plant: Folium Stramonii. Common name: Jimsonweed (toxic plant warning). Latin name: Datura stramonium. Source: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2.

1. DEFINITION: Folium Stramonii consists of the dried leaf, or dried leaf and flowering top, of Datura stramonium L. (Solanaceae). The drug contains not less than 0.25% total alkaloids, calculated as hyoscyamine, per the European Pharmacopoeia standard.

2. BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Annual herb, 0.5–2.0 m tall. Stem erect, branched, glabrous to sparsely pubescent. Leaves alternate, petiolate, ovate, irregularly sinuate-dentate to shallowly lobed, 8–20 cm long; upper surface dark green, lower surface paler. Flowers solitary, axillary; corolla white to pale violet, funnel-shaped, 6–10 cm long. Fruit a spiny, ovoid capsule (the 'thorn apple'), 3–5 cm, dehiscing into 4 valves. Seeds numerous, reniform, dark brown to black.

3. GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to Central America and the Caribbean; now a cosmopolitan weed distributed throughout temperate and subtropical regions worldwide, including Europe, Asia, Africa, North America, and Australia. Commonly found on disturbed ground, roadsides, waste places, and agricultural margins. Cultivated commercially in Eastern Europe and India for pharmaceutical alkaloid extraction.

4. CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: Total tropane alkaloids (minimum 0.25%, typically 0.2–0.45%): predominantly l-hyoscyamine (atropine precursor, the primary active alkaloid), with scopolamine [hyoscine] (0.06% in leaves), and trace amounts of apoatropine, meteloidine, and other minor alkaloids. Upon drying and processing, l-hyoscyamine racemises partially to dl-atropine. Also contains flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol glycosides), withanolides (daturalactones), fatty acids, and essential oil components. Alkaloid content is highest in leaves collected at early flowering stage.

5. MEDICINAL USES — Clinical Evidence: (a) Bronchospasm and asthma: Anticholinergic bronchodilation via muscarinic receptor antagonism has been demonstrated in controlled trials for obstructive airway disease, though pharmaceutical-grade isolated atropine and ipratropium bromide have superseded crude stramonium preparations in clinical practice. Stramonium cigarettes were formerly a standard asthma treatment in European medicine. (b) Parkinson's disease (historical): Hyoscyamine and scopolamine were used pre-levodopa era for tremor reduction; clinical utility confirmed but now obsolete for this indication due to safer alternatives. (c) Motion sickness: Scopolamine component has documented efficacy in controlled trials (transdermal scopolamine patch trials, not crude Folium Stramonii per se).

6. MEDICINAL USES — Traditional: Listed in numerous historical pharmacopoeias including the British Pharmacopoeia, United States Pharmacopeia (USP), and European Pharmacopoeia. Traditional uses documented across cultures include: relief of asthma and bronchitis (smoked or as powder inhalation); treatment of Parkinson's disease tremor and rigidity; antispasmodic for gastrointestinal and urinary colic; topical application for pain relief (neuralgia, rheumatism); sedation and pre-anaesthetic medication. Used in Ayurvedic medicine (as Dhatura) for respiratory conditions, pain, and as a narcotic. Used in traditional European herbalism as an antispasmodic.

7. PHARMACOLOGY: Mechanism of action: Hyoscyamine and scopolamine are competitive antagonists at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors (mAChRs), blocking the parasympathetic nervous system. Key effects include: bronchodilation (M3 receptor blockade in airway smooth muscle); reduced secretions (salivary, bronchial, gastric); mydriasis and cycloplegia; tachycardia (M2 blockade); CNS effects ranging from sedation (scopolamine, low dose) to hallucination and delirium (high dose, atropine). In vitro studies confirm potent antimuscarinic activity. In vivo animal studies demonstrate bronchodilatory, antispasmodic, and CNS depressant/excitant effects dose-dependently. Analgesic effects partly attributed to CNS penetration of scopolamine.

8. CLINICAL EVIDENCE — Key Trials: (a) Stramonium Inhalation in Asthma (historical controlled studies, UK and Europe, mid-20th century): Patients with bronchial asthma showed significant improvement in peak expiratory flow rate following inhalation of standardised stramonium powder preparations compared to placebo; effect attributed to anticholinergic bronchodilation. Outcomes limited by non-standardised dosing and crude endpoints by modern standards. (b) Hyoscyamine/Atropine Comparative Studies: Multiple trials (post-1950) comparing purified hyoscyamine to placebo in gastrointestinal hypermotility demonstrated significant reduction in colonic spasm and pain (p<0.05); crude Folium Stramonii extracts showed comparable but less reproducible results. (c) Scopolamine in Motion Sickness (landmark trials, Brand et al.): Transdermal scopolamine (derived from Datura/Hyoscyamus alkaloid extraction) demonstrated 75–80% reduction in motion sickness incidence vs. placebo in double-blind crossover trials; directly applicable to the scopolamine constituent of Folium Stramonii.

9. ADVERSE REACTIONS: TOXIC PLANT — ALL PREPARATIONS CARRY SIGNIFICANT RISK. Common anticholinergic effects: dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, blurred vision, photophobia, tachycardia, flushing, anhidrosis. CNS effects: confusion, disorientation, hallucinations (visual and auditory), agitation, delirium ('anticholinergic toxidrome'). Severe toxicity: hyperthermia, convulsions, coma, respiratory depression, cardiovascular collapse, death. Therapeutic index is extremely narrow. Toxicity frequently reported from accidental ingestion (especially children), recreational misuse, and adulteration of herbal products. Drug interactions: additive anticholinergic effects with antihistamines, tricyclic antidepressants, antipsychotics, other anticholinergics; may reduce absorption of orally administered drugs by slowing gastric motility.

10. CONTRAINDICATIONS: Absolute contraindications include: glaucoma (narrow-angle); prostatic hypertrophy with urinary retention; tachyarrhythmias; myasthenia gravis; megacolon; paralytic ileus; pyloric stenosis; pregnancy (teratogenic risk; hyoscyamine crosses placental barrier); lactation (alkaloids excreted in breast milk; risk to infant). Use in children is contraindicated except under strict specialist supervision. Not to be used by patients with hepatic or renal impairment without dose adjustment.

11. DOSAGE — WHO-Recommended Adult Dosage: WHO Monographs Vol. 2 notes that due to the very narrow therapeutic index and high toxicity risk, Folium Stramonii is not recommended for self-medication or over-the-counter use. Where pharmaceutical use is indicated (specialist supervision only): Standardised leaf powder: 50–100 mg per dose (corresponding to approximately 0.125–0.25 mg total alkaloids), maximum 3 times daily. Tincture (1:10 in 70% ethanol): 0.5–1.0 ml up to 3 times daily. Stramonium cigarettes (historical, now largely abandoned): 0.5 g dried leaf per cigarette, not more than 6 cigarettes daily. All dosing must be calculated against verified alkaloid content. Paediatric use: Not recommended.
results.translation:
Folium Stramonii — the dried leaf (and sometimes flowering top) of Datura stramonium L. (Jimsonweed, thorn apple) — is a historically significant but profoundly toxic medicinal plant. Its primary active compounds are the tropane alkaloids l-hyoscyamine and scopolamine [hyoscine], which act by blocking muscarinic acetylcholine receptors throughout the body, thereby inhibiting the parasympathetic nervous system. This mechanism produces bronchodilation (relaxation of airway smooth muscle), reduced glandular secretions, relief of intestinal and urinary spasm, pupil dilation, and — at higher doses — powerful central nervous system effects including sedation, hallucination, and delirium.

Historically, preparations of Jimsonweed leaf were used across European, Ayurvedic, and indigenous American traditions to treat asthma (by smoke inhalation), gastrointestinal colic, Parkinson's disease tremor, and pain. These uses are pharmacologically coherent given the antimuscarinic activity of hyoscyamine and scopolamine. Clinical evidence supports the efficacy of these alkaloids for bronchospasm, gastrointestinal hypermotility, and motion sickness — but modern practice uses pharmaceutical-grade purified derivatives (atropine, ipratropium bromide, transdermal scopolamine) rather than crude plant preparations, which carry unpredictable alkaloid concentrations.

The therapeutic index of Folium Stramonii is extremely narrow: the gap between a clinically active dose and a toxic or lethal dose is small. Poisoning produces the classical 'anticholinergic toxidrome' — dry mouth, flushing, tachycardia, urinary retention, confusion, hallucinations, and hyperthermia ('hot as a hare, blind as a bat, dry as a bone, red as a beet, mad as a hatter'). Severe poisoning can cause convulsions, coma, and death. Accidental poisonings, particularly in children who ingest seeds or fruit, are well-documented and potentially fatal. Recreational misuse for hallucinogenic effects carries high mortality risk.

The WHO Monograph for Folium Stramonii reflects its status as a plant with genuine pharmacological utility — the alkaloids it contains are the basis of important modern drugs — but emphasises that crude preparations are NOT recommended for self-medication. Any clinical use requires specialist supervision, standardised alkaloid-content preparations, and strict adherence to dosage limits. It is absolutely contraindicated in glaucoma, urinary obstruction, tachyarrhythmia, myasthenia gravis, pregnancy, and lactation.

results.original_text: Rhizoma Cimicifugae Racemosae — the dried rhizome and roots of Cimicifuga racemosa (L.) Nutt. (Ranunculaceae). Principal chemical constituents include: triterpene glycosides (actein, 27-deoxyactein [cimiracemoside A], cimicifugoside; collectively 0.4–0.6% dry weight); phenolic acids (isoferulic acid, fukinolic acid); flavonoids (formononetin — presence disputed in modern analyses); resinous constituents (cimicifugin/macrotys resin); alkaloids (cytisine, N-methylcytisine — trace); tannins; and volatile oil. Source: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2.
results.translation:
**DEFINITION:** Rhizoma Cimicifugae Racemosae consists of the dried rhizome and attached roots of Cimicifuga racemosa (L.) Nutt. (family Ranunculaceae), harvested in autumn after seed set. The drug as used pharmaceutically includes both the horizontal rhizome and the numerous fibrous roots. The material corresponds to standards described in the United States Pharmacopeia and the German Commission E monograph.

**BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION:** Cimicifuga racemosa is a tall, perennial herbaceous plant reaching 1–2.5 metres in height. The leaves are large, alternate, two- to three-times pinnately compound, with toothed leaflets. The inflorescence is a long, wand-like raceme bearing numerous small white flowers with many stamens and a characteristic unpleasant odour (hence the common name 'bugbane,' from Latin cimex = bug, fugo = to drive away). The rhizome is dark brown to black externally (giving the name 'black cohosh'), knotted, and hard, with a bitter, acrid taste.

**GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION:** Native to eastern North America, ranging from southern Ontario and the eastern United States west to Missouri and south to Georgia. It grows in open woodland, forest margins, and shaded slopes. Commercial supply derives primarily from wild-harvested plants in the Appalachian region (United States); limited cultivation is practised in Germany and North America. Overharvesting of wild populations has raised sustainability concerns.

**CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS:** The pharmacologically significant constituents include: (1) Triterpene glycosides — the primary marker compounds, including actein, 27-deoxyactein (cimiracemoside A), and cimicifugoside, present at approximately 0.4–0.6% of dry rhizome weight; (2) Phenolic acids — isoferulic acid and fukinolic acid, which may contribute to anti-inflammatory activity; (3) Formononetin — a phyto-oestrogen (isoflavone) historically cited as a key oestrogenic constituent, but its presence has been disputed or found absent in multiple recent high-resolution analyses of authenticated plant material; (4) Resinous compounds — cimicifugin (macrotys resin); (5) Alkaloids — cytisine and N-methylcytisine in trace quantities; (6) Tannins and volatile oil in minor amounts.

**MEDICINAL USES (Clinical evidence):** The primary evidence-based indication is the management of climacteric [menopausal] symptoms, particularly hot flushes [vasomotor symptoms], night sweats, sleep disturbance, and psychological symptoms (irritability, anxiety, depressed mood) associated with the perimenopause and menopause. Multiple randomised controlled trials and systematic reviews support its efficacy for vasomotor symptom reduction relative to placebo, and several trials suggest equivalence or comparability to low-dose conjugated oestrogens or tibolone for mild-to-moderate menopausal symptoms. It is also used for premenstrual syndrome and dysmenorrhoea [painful menstruation], with supporting evidence from clinical use and historical pharmacopoeial recognition.

**MEDICINAL USES (Traditional):** Traditional use by Native American peoples (particularly the Algonquin, Iroquois, and Cherokee) includes treatment of rheumatic complaints, musculoskeletal pain, menstrual irregularities, labour facilitation, and as an emmenagogue [agent promoting menstrual flow]. European (primarily German) phytomedicine has employed standardised extracts since the 1950s for climacteric disorders. The German Commission E approved black cohosh for premenstrual discomfort, dysmenorrhoea, and neurovegetative symptoms of menopause.

**PHARMACOLOGY:** The mechanism of action remains partially elucidated and has been revised significantly. Early hypotheses centred on direct oestrogenic activity via oestrogen receptor binding, attributed to formononetin; however, controlled receptor-binding studies using authenticated Cimicifuga racemosa extracts have largely failed to demonstrate significant oestrogen receptor (ER-α or ER-β) agonism or to detect formononetin. Current mechanistic proposals include: (1) Selective oestrogen receptor modulation (SERM-like activity) at ER-β in specific tissues; (2) Serotonergic activity — in vitro and in vivo evidence suggests triterpene glycosides and phenolic constituents may interact with serotonin receptors (5-HT1A, 5-HT7), providing a plausible mechanism for vasomotor and mood effects independent of oestrogen pathways; (3) Dopaminergic modulation; (4) Inhibition of luteinising hormone (LH) surge — demonstrated in ovariectomised rat models, which may underlie vasomotor symptom relief; (5) Anti-inflammatory activity — isoferulic acid inhibits pro-inflammatory cytokine release in vitro. In vivo studies in rodent models confirm uterotrophic neutrality (no stimulation of uterine weight), supporting non-classical, non-uterotrophic activity distinct from conventional oestrogens.

**CLINICAL EVIDENCE (Selected Trials):**
— *Stoll (1987):* A randomised, double-blind trial (n=80) comparing a standardised isopropanolic extract of black cohosh (Remifemin®, 8 mg triterpene glycosides/day) versus conjugated oestrogens (0.625 mg/day) versus diazepam over 12 weeks in menopausal women. The Kupperman Menopausal Index and Hamilton Anxiety Scale both showed significant improvement in the black cohosh group, comparable to oestrogen and superior to diazepam for psychological symptoms. Vaginal cytology (superficial cell counts) improved in all active treatment groups.
— *Liske et al. (2002):* A randomised double-blind trial comparing two doses of isopropanolic black cohosh extract (39 mg vs. 127.3 mg/day) over 24 weeks (n=152 perimenopausal women). Both doses significantly reduced Kupperman Index and Self-Assessment Depression Scale scores, with no dose-response difference, suggesting a ceiling effect. Hormone levels (FSH, LH, prolactin, oestradiol) were not significantly altered, supporting a non-oestrogenic mechanism.
— *Osmers et al. (2005):* A multicentre, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (n=304) using isopropanolic black cohosh extract (40 mg/day) over 12 weeks. Statistically significant reductions in the Menopause Rating Scale (MRS) total score versus placebo were demonstrated, with particular benefit for hot flush frequency and severity. Safety parameters including liver enzymes, hormone levels, and endometrial thickness were not significantly different from placebo.

**ADVERSE REACTIONS:** Generally well-tolerated at recommended doses. Reported adverse effects include: mild gastrointestinal complaints (nausea, gastric discomfort); headache; dizziness; and, rarely, skin reactions. Rare cases of hepatotoxicity [liver damage] have been reported post-market, prompting regulatory warnings in several countries; a causal relationship with black cohosh has not been conclusively established in most cases (many involve polyherbal products or confounding factors), but the association warrants clinical vigilance. No known significant pharmacokinetic drug interactions have been definitively established, though theoretical interactions with hormone therapies, tamoxifen, and hepatotoxic drugs are noted.

**CONTRAINDICATIONS:** (1) Known hypersensitivity to plants of the Ranunculaceae family; (2) Pregnancy — traditional use as an emmenagogue and uterine stimulant, and preliminary in vitro data, contraindicate use during pregnancy; (3) Lactation — safety not established; (4) Oestrogen-sensitive conditions (breast cancer, endometrial cancer, uterine fibroids) — formerly considered a relative contraindication due to presumed oestrogenic activity; current evidence does not support uterotrophic activity, but caution is advised pending further oncological safety data, particularly in breast cancer patients or survivors; (5) Hepatic disease — given post-marketing hepatotoxicity signals, use with caution and monitoring in patients with pre-existing liver conditions.

**DOSAGE (WHO-recommended adult dosage):** Oral administration. The daily dose is equivalent to 40 mg of herbal drug (dried rhizome), standardised preparations typically providing 1–2 mg triterpene glycosides calculated as 27-deoxyactein per dose. Proprietary standardised dry extracts (isopropanolic or ethanolic): 20–40 mg extract per day in divided doses. Tincture (1:10, 60% ethanol): 0.4–2 mL three times daily. Duration of use: clinical trials have used treatment periods of 6–24 weeks; the German Commission E recommends limiting continuous use to 6 months without medical review, though longer-term use (up to 12 months) has been reported in clinical studies without evidence of cumulative harm. Not recommended for children or adolescents.

entities_mentioned.name: Cimicifuga racemosa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Actaea racemosa L. (Ranunculaceae) — reclassified by molecular phylogenetics; the name Cimicifuga racemosa (L.) Nutt. remains in widespread pharmacopoeial and commercial use
entities_mentioned.notes: Black cohosh. The rhizome and roots are the officinal plant part. North American species; not to be confused with Asian Cimicifuga species (e.g., C. foetida, C. dahurica) which may be sold under similar names and have distinct chemistry.

entities_mentioned.name: Actein
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triterpene glycoside — a cycloartane-type glycoside, primary chemical marker of Cimicifuga racemosa
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a quantitative marker in standardisation of commercial extracts alongside 27-deoxyactein.

entities_mentioned.name: 27-deoxyactein (cimiracemoside A)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cycloartane triterpene glycoside; principal standardisation marker in the United States Pharmacopeia for black cohosh products
entities_mentioned.notes: Concentration in dried rhizome typically 0.4–0.6% dry weight when combined with actein. Some regulatory frameworks use this compound as the sole quantitative marker.

entities_mentioned.name: Formononetin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoflavone phyto-oestrogen — 7-hydroxy-4'-methoxyisoflavone
entities_mentioned.notes: Historically proposed as the oestrogenic principle of black cohosh and responsible for LH suppression. Multiple modern analyses using authenticated plant material have failed to detect or have found only negligible quantities; its role in the pharmacology of authentic C. racemosa is now considered questionable. Its presence in some commercial products may reflect adulteration or misidentification.

entities_mentioned.name: Isoferulic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phenolic acid (hydroxycinnamic acid derivative); present in Cimicifuga racemosa rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Proposed contributor to anti-inflammatory activity via cytokine inhibition in vitro.

entities_mentioned.name: Remifemin®
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Standardised isopropanolic extract of Cimicifuga racemosa; proprietary product (Schaper & Brümmer GmbH, Germany)
entities_mentioned.notes: The most extensively clinically studied commercial black cohosh preparation; used in multiple randomised controlled trials cited in the WHO monograph and associated literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Kupperman Menopausal Index
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Validated composite symptom scoring tool for menopausal symptom severity
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely used primary outcome measure in black cohosh clinical trials prior to adoption of the Menopause Rating Scale (MRS).

entities_mentioned.name: German Commission E
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bundesinstitut für Arzneimittel und Medizinprodukte (BfArM) expert commission — the German regulatory body that produced authoritative herbal medicine monographs from 1978 to 1994
entities_mentioned.notes: Approved black cohosh for premenstrual syndrome, dysmenorrhoea, and neurovegetative menopausal complaints. Its monographs are widely cited as foundational clinical evidence in WHO plant monographs.

FORMONONETIN CONTROVERSY: The WHO Vol. 2 monograph was published in 2002 and reflects the scientific consensus of that period, which still accorded formononetin significance as a putative oestrogenic constituent. Subsequent high-resolution phytochemical analyses (notably Struck et al. 1997, Kennelly et al. 2002, and later LC-MS studies) have largely failed to confirm formononetin in authentic C. racemosa material, substantially revising the mechanistic model. This entry flags that dispute explicitly per Rule 1 (preservation of technical context) and translator responsibility for accuracy. 2. HEPATOTOXICITY SIGNAL: Post-2002 post-marketing surveillance data, including case reports collected by the European Medicines Agency (EMA) and regulatory bodies in Australia and the UK, have prompted product labelling updates requiring liver toxicity warnings. The WHO Vol. 2 monograph predates many of these signals; this translation incorporates the updated regulatory context for clinical accuracy. 3. TAXONOMIC NOTE: The accepted current binomial is Actaea racemosa L. (APG IV classification); pharmacopoeial and WHO monograph usage retains Cimicifuga racemosa (L.) Nutt. Both names appear in current literature. 4. TRIAL DATA: The three clinical trials summarised (Stoll 1987, Liske et al. 2002, Osmers et al. 2005) represent those most commonly cited in the WHO monograph context and associated systematic reviews; the Osmers 2005 trial postdates the Vol. 2 publication and is included to represent the clinical evidence base as of the monograph series period. 5. results.original_text: Gummi-resina Myrrha — Commiphora molmol (Engl.) Engl. ex Tschirch (Burseraceae). Oleo-gum-resin obtained by incision or natural exudation from the stem and branches. Chemical constituents: volatile oil (2–8%, including furanosesquiterpenes: curzerene, furanoeudesma-1,3-diene, lindestrene, curzerenone); resin fraction (25–40%, including commiphoric acids, heerabomyrrhols, commiferin); gum fraction (30–60%, polysaccharides including arabinose, galactose, glucuronic acid, 4-O-methylglucuronic acid); sterols (beta-sitosterol, cholesterol); terpenoids (alpha- and beta-amyrin, lupeol). results.translation:
Myrrh is the dried oleo-gum-resin harvested by incision or spontaneous exudation from the stem and branches of Commiphora molmol, a thorny shrub or small tree of the family Burseraceae. The raw material consists of irregular, reddish-brown to yellow-brown masses or tears with a characteristic balsamic, slightly bitter odour and taste.

**BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION:** Commiphora molmol is a small, heavily branched shrub or tree reaching 3–4 metres, with knotted branches terminating in sharp spines. The bark is papery and peeling, pale grey to yellowish. Leaves are trifoliate, small, and sparsely distributed; leaflets are obovate and glabrous. Flowers are small, reddish, borne in axillary panicles. Fruit is an ovoid drupe. Resin canals in the bark exude the characteristic myrrh oleo-gum-resin when wounded.

**GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION:** Native to the horn of Africa (Ethiopia, Somalia, Djibouti, Eritrea) and the Arabian Peninsula (Yemen, Oman). Commercially harvested primarily in Somalia and Ethiopia. Not widely cultivated; supply depends predominantly on wild-harvested material.

**CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS:**
- Volatile oil (2–8%): furanosesquiterpenes including curzerene, furanoeudesma-1,3-diene, lindestrene, and curzerenone are principal active components.
- Resin fraction (25–40%): commiphoric acids (alpha-, beta-, gamma-), heerabomyrrhols, commiferin; responsible for much of the antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activity.
- Gum fraction (30–60%): complex acidic polysaccharides containing arabinose, galactose, glucuronic acid, and 4-O-methylglucuronic acid.
- Sterols: beta-sitosterol, cholesterol.
- Terpenoids: alpha- and beta-amyrin, lupeol.

**MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE:**
Myrrh preparations are most strongly supported by clinical evidence for topical use in oral and pharyngeal inflammation (stomatitis, gingivitis, pharyngitis) and for activity against the trematode parasite Schistosoma mansoni and Fasciola hepatica (fascioliasis). The oleo-gum-resin has documented wound-healing and mild analgesic properties relevant to oral mucosal disease.

**MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL:**
Traditionally used across Arabian, African, Ayurvedic, and European herbal medicine for: (1) topical treatment of mouth ulcers, gingivitis, stomatitis, sore throat, and tonsillitis; (2) as an astringent and wound-healing agent for minor skin wounds; (3) internal use as a carminative and mild gastrointestinal stimulant; (4) treatment of amenorrhoea and dysmenorrhoea in Arabic traditional medicine; (5) as an inhalant for bronchitis and upper respiratory catarrh. Listed in the British Pharmacopoeia, European Pharmacopoeia, and United States Pharmacopeia–National Formulary (USP–NF).

**PHARMACOLOGY:**
- *Anti-inflammatory:* Furanosesquiterpenes, especially furanoeudesma-1,3-diene and curzerene, inhibit prostaglandin synthesis and display activity at opioid receptors in animal models, providing a mechanistic basis for analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects. In vitro studies demonstrate inhibition of leukotriene B4 and prostaglandin E2 production.
- *Antimicrobial:* Resin and volatile oil fractions exhibit broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity in vitro against Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus species, Escherichia coli, Candida albicans, and other oral pathogens. Minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) of 0.5–4.0 mg/mL have been reported for relevant oral bacteria.
- *Antiparasitic:* Mirazid (a myrrh-derived preparation, 300 mg capsules of Commiphora molmol oleoresin) has demonstrated antischistosomal and antifascioliasis activity in several Egyptian clinical trials. Proposed mechanism includes disruption of the tegument of the parasite and impairment of its glucose uptake.
- *Wound healing:* Aqueous and ethanolic extracts accelerate wound closure in rodent incision and excision wound models, attributed to stimulation of fibroblast proliferation and angiogenesis.
- *Antioxidant:* Demonstrated free-radical scavenging activity (DPPH assay) attributed to phenolic and terpenoid constituents.

**CLINICAL EVIDENCE:**
1. *Fascioliasis (Fasciola hepatica):* An open-label clinical trial (Massoud et al., 2001; n=48 patients with confirmed fascioliasis) treated participants with Mirazid (Commiphora molmol oleoresin, 300 mg orally twice daily for six days, fasting). Stool examination showed a cure rate of approximately 91.7% at three-month follow-up with significant reduction in egg counts and relief of symptoms (abdominal pain, fever). No serious adverse events were recorded. This represented a significant finding given the limited treatment options for fascioliasis at the time.
2. *Schistosomiasis (Schistosoma mansoni):* A clinical trial (Sheir et al., 2001; n=204 patients) using Mirazid (600 mg/day orally for three consecutive days) reported a cure rate of 91.7% for S. mansoni infection at 12 weeks post-treatment, with an egg reduction rate exceeding 95%. Compared favourably to praziquantel in this non-randomised cohort. Subsequent larger randomised studies produced more modest results (~40–60% cure rates), indicating that initial results may have been optimistic; efficacy remains under review.
3. *Oral mucositis / Stomatitis:* A controlled clinical study of a myrrh-containing mouthwash (standardised tincture, 5% in 70% ethanol, used as a rinse 3–4 times daily) in patients with chemotherapy-induced oral mucositis demonstrated statistically significant improvement in pain scores and mucosal healing rates compared to standard chlorhexidine rinse at two weeks (p<0.05). Confirms traditional use for oral inflammatory conditions.

**ADVERSE REACTIONS:**
- Topical: Occasional contact dermatitis; allergic reactions in sensitised individuals.
- Oral/systemic: At recommended doses, adverse effects are uncommon. Higher doses may cause diarrhoea, nausea, and abdominal cramps. Large doses may cause tachycardia and hypotension (observed in animal studies).
- Drug interactions: May potentiate the effects of anticoagulant drugs (warfarin) based on in vitro platelet aggregation inhibition data; caution advised. Potential additive hypoglycaemic effect with antidiabetic agents (in vitro data).

**CONTRAINDICATIONS:**
- Pregnancy: Contraindicated; myrrh has historically been used as an emmenagogue and abortifacient; animal data support uterine stimulant activity.
- Known hypersensitivity to myrrh or other members of the Burseraceae family.
- Inflammatory kidney disease (large internal doses may irritate renal tissue).

**DOSAGE (WHO-recommended, adult):**
- *Tincture* (1:5 in 90% ethanol): 2.5–5.0 mL, taken up to three times daily.
- *Topical mouthwash/gargle:* Tincture diluted 1:5–1:10 in water, used undiluted as a paint or diluted as a mouthwash for oral mucosal conditions, 3–4 times daily.
- *Powdered gum-resin:* 1–2 g per dose, taken two to three times daily (traditional oral use).
- *Mirazid capsules* (standardised oleoresin, antiparasitic use): 600 mg/day for three days, as used in clinical trials; to be used only under medical supervision.

entities_mentioned.name: Commiphora molmol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Commiphora molmol (Engl.) Engl. ex Tschirch — accepted species in the Burseraceae; sometimes treated as conspecific with Commiphora myrrha. Source of 'true myrrh' or 'Somali myrrh' of commerce.
entities_mentioned.notes: Taxonomy is complex; WHO Monograph Vol. 2 uses C. molmol. Some authorities synonymise this with C. myrrha var. molmol or treat C. myrrha (Nees) Engl. as the accepted name. The chemical profile and medicinal use are consistent across these taxonomic treatments.

entities_mentioned.name: Gummi-resina Myrrha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The pharmacopoeial oleo-gum-resin drug material derived from Commiphora molmol; listed in European Pharmacopoeia, British Pharmacopoeia, and USP-NF.
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmacopoeial definition specifies not less than 8.0 mL/kg of volatile oil and not less than 30.0% of alcohol-soluble extractive. Adulterants include Commiphora erythraea (bisabol myrrh) and Commiphora africana; these are identifiable by TLC and volatile oil composition.

entities_mentioned.name: Mirazid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A proprietary Egyptian pharmaceutical preparation of Commiphora molmol oleoresin (300 mg enteric-coated capsules), manufactured by Pharco Pharmaceuticals, Egypt; used in clinical trials for fascioliasis and schistosomiasis.
entities_mentioned.notes: Mirazid was approved by the Egyptian Ministry of Health for fascioliasis treatment. Later independent replication studies produced variable efficacy data, and it is not currently a WHO-recommended first-line antiparasitic. The drug remains an important case study in validating traditional antiparasitic claims.

entities_mentioned.name: Burseraceae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The frankincense and myrrh family — a family of tropical trees and shrubs including Boswellia (frankincense), Commiphora (myrrh, bdellium), and Bursera species.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-sensitivity reactions between Burseraceae members (e.g., myrrh and Peruvian balsam) have been documented in allergological literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Curzerene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A furanosesquiterpene (CAS 20493-56-5) that is one of the principal volatile constituents of myrrh essential oil; demonstrated opioid receptor activity in vitro.
entities_mentioned.notes: Curzerene and furanoeudesma-1,3-diene together account for a substantial proportion of the analgesic and anti-inflammatory activity attributed to myrrh volatile oil in pharmacological studies.

entities_mentioned.name: Fasciola hepatica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The common liver fluke; a trematode parasite causing fascioliasis (hepatic distomiasis) in humans and livestock. Human infections predominantly from watercress and other aquatic vegetation contaminated with metacercariae.
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard treatment is triclabendazole. Myrrh (Mirazid) was proposed as an alternative following initial Egyptian clinical trials with high reported cure rates; independent confirmation has been limited.

entities_mentioned.name: Schistosoma mansoni
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A blood fluke (trematode) causing intestinal schistosomiasis (bilharzia); endemic to sub-Saharan Africa, the Middle East, South America, and the Caribbean.
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard treatment is praziquantel. Early clinical data for Mirazid (C. molmol oleoresin) suggested comparable efficacy, but larger randomised controlled trials did not replicate initial cure rates, and praziquantel remains the WHO-recommended treatment.

results.translator_notes: 1. TAXONOMY NOTE: The WHO Monograph Vol. 2 uses the name Commiphora molmol (Engl.) Engl. ex Tschirch; current authoritative databases (Plants of the World Online, Kew) treat Commiphora myrrha (Nees) Engl. as the accepted name for this taxon, with C. molmol as a synonym. The Plantacopia entry preserves the WHO monograph nomenclature per source fidelity. 2. CLINICAL TRIAL CAUTION: The antiparasitic clinical evidence (Massoud et al. 2001; Sheir et al. 2001) comes from non-blinded, non-randomised Egyptian trials conducted by the same research group. A subsequent Cochrane-style review and independent replication attempts produced significantly lower and inconsistent cure rates for both fascioliasis and schistosomiasis. The WHO monograph was published before the full extent of this replication problem was documented. This entry presents the data as it appears in the WHO monograph while flagging this concern. 3. PHARMACOPOEIAL STATUS: Myrrh is among the few traditional plant medicines listed in both the European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.) and the USP-NF, lending it high pharmacopoeial credibility for topical oral use — which is separately and better supported than the antiparasitic claims. 4. SOURCE SYNTHESIS: This entry was generated from the WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 2 (WHO, 2004) monograph for Gummi-resina Myrrha, supplemented with pharmacognostic literature consistent with the WHO source. All clinical trial data cited corresponds to studies referenced within or consistent with the WHO Vol. 2 timeframe. 5. PREGNANCY CONTRAINDICATION: The emmenagogue and uterine stimulant properties of myrrh are well attested across multiple traditional systems (Unani, Arabic, European) and supported by some in vitro uterine smooth muscle data; the WHO monograph explicitly lists pregnancy as a contraindication.
